narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 49% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your lower Year 1 school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical student's 5.7—and an even lower Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the average around 5.5. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 also falls short of the usual 3.2, and your LSAT score of 35 is slightly under the average 36.7 that stronger applicants bring. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket, being a Native American male pursuing full-time studies, you stand out from the dataset's majority, where most students are White, from higher income groups, and full-time like you but with better academic stats. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your Year 3 decile, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting bar failure since it signals consistently weak performance relative to peers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, reinforcing that early struggles carried weight. Your family income level followed closely, as lower brackets in the data correlate with higher failure risks. Your LSAT score and racial background as Native American—rarer at just 1.8% of applicants—also pushed in the same direction, as did your undergraduate GPA. Gender as male and full-time status had the least sway but still tilted slightly toward the failure prediction. Compared to typical students who pass, your academics are in the bottom ranks across the board, making you a higher-risk profile in the model's eyes. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to aim above 37, build a stronger academic record if pursuing further studies, and invest in targeted bar exam prep courses proven to help students from similar backgrounds succeed. With dedication to these steps, you could shift your profile toward the passing side that the model favors. Keep pushing—you have the drive to rewrite this story.
